entry.title: Aligning privacy incentives in P2MR
entry.summary:
The email highlights a significant issue concerning the security of Bitcoin, particularly in relation to its layer one (L1) and any layer two (L2) solutions built upon it. The primary argument made is that without Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC), Bitcoin's foundational security remains vulnerable, irrespective of the security measures adopted by subsequent L2 solutions. This vulnerability stems from the inherent weakness that if L2 solutions settle transactions back to Bitcoin's original blockchain, they inherit its security limitations.
The discussion further elaborates on two potential scenarios for Bitcoin as it approaches what is referred to as "Q-day," the hypothetical point when quantum computing could effectively break Bitcoin’s current cryptographic defenses. In the first scenario, if Bitcoin integrates hash-based PQC before Q-day, the addition of complexity and security assumptions remains minimal. This approach would utilize algorithms developed in the 1970s that are designed to work with existing hash functions already used in Bitcoin. Conversely, the second scenario outlines a more dire situation where, if no PQC solution is implemented prior to Q-day, Bitcoin may have to adopt ZK-STARK circuits. These circuits are significantly more complex and potentially more bug-prone than hash-based algorithms, as evidenced by recent vulnerabilities found in similar technologies like Zcash.
The urgency conveyed in the message suggests that the longer the Bitcoin community delays addressing the quantum computing threat, the more complicated and potentially insecure any post-Q-day solutions will have to be. This adds a layer of criticality to the development and implementation timelines for PQC within Bitcoin's infrastructure. The discussion serves as a call to action for the community to prioritize these developments to secure the future integrity of Bitcoin transactions.
